data.title: U.S. may use emergency authority to fire up retired coal plants, Burgum says
data.description:
Coal-powered plants may see a revival under the Trump administration, as U.S. Interior Secretary Doug Burgum said the U.S. may use emergency authority to bring back coal-fired plants that have closed and stop others from shutting down, action he said is needed to help the U.S. meet rising electricity demand that is being fueled by a boom in artificial intelligence.

"Under the national energy emergency, which President Trump has declared, we've got to keep every coal plant open," Burgum told Bloomberg in an interview at the CERAWeek energy conference in Houston. "And if there had been units at a coal plant that have been shut down, we need to bring those back.

Burgum said the U.S. also could keep existing plants open by streamlining environmental regulations imposed by previous presidential administrations.

"We can stop death by regulation," the secretary said. "Part of that we can do by taking a close look at some of the actual legality of some of the rule-making that was perpetrated against these industries."

Coal generated more than half of U.S. power at the beginning of the century, but in 2023, the Energy Information Administration estimated that coal comprised 16% of total U.S. net electricity generation, with nuclear and renewables surpassing it coal at 21% and 18% respectively.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:XLU), (XLE)

data.title: Rio Tinto raises $9B for Arcadium Lithium buyout - Bloomberg
data.description:
Rio Tinto (NYSE:RIO) sold $9B of U.S. investment-grade bonds, as the miner raised funds for its recently-closed buyout of Arcadium Lithium, Bloomberg reported Tuesday.

The debt was sold in eight parts, including 40-year bonds that yield 1.33 percentage point more than U.S. Treasuries, according to the report, which also said investors placed orders for more than $50B of bonds at their peak, a sign of the strength of demand.

Rio Tinto (NYSE:RIO) last week completed its $6.7B purchase of Arcadium, a deal that initially was funded by bridge financing and intended to be replaced by longer-term debt; the company had looked at selling as much as $5B of equity to help pay for the acqusition, but reportedly scrapped those plans.

Rio Tinto (RIO) was among 11 issuers that sold U.S. investment-grade debt Tuesday, Bloomberg reported, even as intensifying tariff fights pressured stocks and lifted U.S. Treasury prices.

data.title: Venture Global bounces as co-founders buy shares after selloff
data.description:
Venture Global (NYSE:VG) closed +8% on Tuesday after its co-founders snapped up millions of dollars worth of shares, following a drop in the company’s stock to record lows after one of the worst-ever U.S. IPOs, Bloomberg reported.

Company founders Mike Sabel and Bob Pender each bought $2.34M worth of stock during Monday’s selloff, according to SEC filings, as losses since the liquefied natural gas exporter went public topped 60%, but shares rallied as much as 11% Tuesday even as the broader U.S. equity market again fell sharply.

Venture Global (NYSE:VG) shares have plunged since January's IPO, and the slide accelerated last week as the company reported lackluster results and added $2B to the estimated project cost for its Plaquemines liquefied natural gas plant, less than seven weeks after being on the road to pitch itself to would-be buyers.

data.title: Crude oil bumps up from six-month low but near-term risks still seen tilted to downside
data.description:
Crude oil futures edged higher Tuesday despite intraday selling pressure as the increasingly nasty trade dispute between the U.S. and Canada heated up and then cooled down, with Ontario Premier Doug Ford agreeing to suspend a 25% surcharge on electricity exports to the U.S. after President Trump said he would double the tariff on Canadian steel and aluminum and steel.

Prices pared some of their earlier gains following reports that Ukraine had agreed to a 30-day cease-fire agreement in its conflict with Russia, which helped improve the sour mood in the stock market, but for oil, a deal eventually could lead to a rise in global supplies that could pressure prices.

"Near-term risks remain tilted to the downside,” Forex.com analyst Fawad Razaqzada said, noting tariff risks for demand, OPEC+ production plans, and the process to end the Russia-Ukraine war, but unless a major global downturn emerges, “the downside is likely to be limited for oil from here, and WTI could potentially bottom around the $60 area should it get there."

A scaling back of U.S. tariffs would ease fears of inflation and economic contraction, but oil's recent plunge means it is "hard to see OPEC+ going ahead with its plan and releasing oil back to the market from April," PVM analyst Tamas Varga said.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for April delivery finished +0.3% to $66.25/bbl, and front-month May Brent crude (CO1:COM) closed +0.4% to $69.56/bbl, after both benchmarks settled Monday at their lowest levels since September 10.

U.S. natural gas futures (NG1:COM) ended +1.5% to $4.558/MMBtu, with the market showing little concern about warming March temperature forecasts that suggest a bearish tail end of the winter storage withdrawal season.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG)

Despite the weakening economic outlook that has weighed on futures prices, WTI's prompt spread - a key indicator of near-term supply and demand balances - has held steady in a bullish, backwardated structure, which is a sign that the growth scare for crude is less severe than for other assets, Strategas analyst Jon Byrne told Bloomberg.

"Crude could be on the cusp of decoupling from other risk assets during this selloff," Byrne said.

"Given how light positioning is, it doesn't take much to move the market," CIBC Private Wealth Group's Rebecca Babin said. "In my view, these are all noisy headlines that can fuel a short-term bounce, but the real economic data remains concerning, which will ultimately keep crude under pressure."

data.title: Rocket Lab to acquire Mynaric
data.description:
Rocket Lab USA (NASDAQ:RKLB) intends to acquire a controlling interest in Mynaric AG, a leading provider of laser optical communications terminals for air, space, and mobile applications, and an existing subcontractor to Rocket Lab (NASDAQ:RKLB).

By acquiring a majority of Mynaric, Rocket Lab (NASDAQ:RKLB) will strengthen its capabilities as a spacecraft manufacturer, launch provider, and supplier of satellite components at scale, and will provide the company with intellectual property, and product inventory related to satellite-to-satellite optical connectivity for next generation constellations.

“Rocket Lab is pursuing every part of the space value chain,” said CEO Peter Beck. “And now we’re closing in on the final step and most valuable part of the space economy – operating our own constellations to provide data and services from space using our newly announced Flatellite spacecraft."

Under the terms of the deal, Rocket Lab (RKLB) would acquire 100% of the outstanding equity interest in Mynaric after the StaRUG restructuring with an initial purchase price of $75M with the potential for additional earn-out consideration based on future Mynaric revenue targets.

The company may fund this acquisition with proceeds from a previously announced equity offering.

Press Release

data.title: Gold price rises as tariff wars spark economic slowdown worries
data.description:
Front-month gold gained Tuesday, with markets on edge due to President Trump's trade policies, which are contributing to heightened economic uncertainty and a weaker dollar, with the U.S. dollar index sliding to its lowest level since mid-October.

Trump said in a social media post this morning that he would double proposed tariffs on Canadian steel and aluminum to 50%, but later restored the 25% rate after Ontario Premier Doug Ford said he will suspend the province's 25% surtax on U.S.-bound electricity.

The latest exchange came just hours before broader global 25% tariffs were due to come into effect Wednesday on steel and aluminum; the Trump administration has said repeatedly that there would be no exemptions, and it appears to be sticking with it in internal discussions and conversations with industry and labor groups, at least for now.

"Gold has been consolidating in the midst of mayhem within striking distance of the all-time high," positioned to set new records as the U.S. and Canada trade blows over tariffs, Mizuho's Robert Yawger said, adding that even as investors seem hesitant to keep pushing gold up, "it is still only one big day away from ringing the bell."

Market attention will turn to Wednesday's U.S. Consumer Price Index and Thursday's Producer Price Index.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:GLD), (NYSEARCA:GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

Front-month Comex gold for March delivery (XAUUSD:CUR) closed +0.7% to $2,912.90/oz, and front-month March silver settled +1.9% to $32.888/bbl, with both metals snapping a two-session losing streak.

"Gold is likely to remain supported amid ongoing market uncertainties, bolstering demand for the safe-haven asset. However, any positive developments in Russia-Ukraine negotiations could reduce risk premiums," OANDA analyst Zain Vawda said.

After regular Comex trading closed, the U.S. said it will resume military aid and intelligence sharing with Ukraine after talks where Ukraine said it would accept a U.S. proposal for a 30-day ceasefire; Secretary of State Rubio said the U.S. would now take the offer to Russia, and that the ball is in Moscow's court.
